questions.prompt: What does the rule 'Disallow: /admin/' in a robots.txt file actually do?

choices.text: Tells search engines not to index pages under /admin/.
Tells search engines not to crawl pages under /admin/. They may still appear in search results as bare URLs if linked from elsewhere.
Hides the /admin/ section from users entirely.

questions.explanation: robots.txt controls crawling only — not indexing, not user access. Blocked URLs can still show up in Google's results (with no snippet) if other sites link to them. To truly keep a page out of search results, use a noindex meta tag and leave the URL crawlable so Google can see the noindex.

questions.prompt: Your site starts returning HTTP 500 errors during a traffic surge. After the surge ends, you notice Googlebot is still crawling far less often than it used to. Why?

choices.text: Google permanently lowered your crawl budget as a penalty.
Googlebot reads server errors as a 'slow down' signal and reduces crawl rate. It cautiously eases back up over days or weeks as the server proves it's healthy again.
You need to submit a manual reconsideration request.
The crawl rate is determined only by your sitemap, so HTTP errors don't matter.

questions.explanation: Google explicitly says it slows crawling when it sees errors so it doesn't make your problem worse. Recovery is gradual — it isn't an instant snap-back when your site stabilizes. The fix is to keep your server healthy, not to manually 'reset' anything.
